food_name: Nem Nuong Sandwich (Banh Mi Nem Nuong)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Banh Mi Nem Nuong. Closest parent is the generic sandwich food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Sandwich) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Vietnamese-style nem nuong sandwich.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific dish. Generic sandwich concepts do not adequately represent this Vietnamese-Singaporean hybrid food.

serving.natural_language_portion: One nem nuong sandwich (approximately 280 g) consisting of a French baguette filled with grilled Vietnamese pork sausage, pork pate, mayonnaise, pickled vegetables, cucumber, cilantro, chilli, and fish sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Banh Mi (Vietnamese Sandwich) — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central entries for French bread, grilled pork sausage, pork liver pate, mayonnaise, pickled vegetables, and fresh herbs
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Banh Mi Nem Nuong as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: French bread (FDC 172688), pork sausage grilled (FDC 167903), pork liver pate (FDC 174607), mayonnaise (FDC 172741), pickled daikon/carrot (FDC 169977 proxy), cucumber (FDC 168409), fresh cilantro (FDC 169997), fish sauce (FDC 175148). Proportions estimated from standard banh mi construction: baguette ~35%, pork sausage ~25%, pate ~8%, mayonnaise ~7%, pickled veg ~12%, fresh veg/herbs ~8%, condiments ~5%. Per-100g values derived from weighted average of assembled 280g serving.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white bread and baguette reference; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Banh Mi Nem Nuong. GI estimated at 62 based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being a French baguette (GI ~70 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the substantial protein (pork sausage, pate), fat (mayonnaise, pate), and dietary fibre (pickled vegetables, cucumber, cilantro) content of the filling, which collectively lower the composite meal GI. Analogous composite sandwich GI values from Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care) support a medium GI range of 55–65 for filled baguette-style sandwiches with protein and fat-rich fillings. GL per serving = (62 × 63.0 g carbohydrate per 280g serving) / 100 = 39.1, rounded to 38.7 after fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, pork pate, and processed pork sausage
hypertension.guidance: A single nem nuong sandwich provides approximately 1,456 mg sodium (520 mg per 100g × 280g serving), representing over 60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce and pate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by refined white baguette carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 62 and a glycemic load of approximately 39 per serving, this sandwich poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control, choosing a smaller baguette, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help attenuate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork pate, pork sausage, and mayonnaise combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (approximately 8.1 g per serving) from pork pate and mayonnaise, together with high sodium content, warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; occasional consumption is acceptable but regular intake should be moderated in the context of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banh Mi Nem Nuong (Bánh Mì Nem Nướng) is a Vietnamese-style filled baguette sandwich served in Singapore, most notably at Co Chung Restaurant on Lorong Telok. The sandwich features a crisp French baguette filled with layers of homemade mayonnaise, smooth pork liver pate, grilled nem nuong (Vietnamese-style minced pork sausages seasoned with garlic, lemongrass, and fish sauce), pickled daikon and carrot, fresh cucumber slices, cilantro, sliced chilli, and a drizzle of special fish sauce-based dipping sauce. Nem nuong are characteristically bouncy and slightly smoky in texture with a sweet-savoury flavour profile. The sandwich delivers a complex interplay of textures — crisp baguette crust, tender grilled sausage, creamy pate, tangy pickles, and fresh herbs — making it a highly satisfying meal. Nutritionally, the sandwich provides approximately 218 kcal per 100g, with meaningful protein from the pork sausage and pate, moderate fat from mayonnaise and pate, and carbohydrate primarily from the baguette. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce, pate, and processed pork, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The medium glycemic index (approximately 62) reflects the refined baguette base moderated by protein and fat in the filling. This dish is representative of the Vietnamese culinary influence on Singapore's food culture and is a popular lunch and dinner option.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Banh Mi Nem Nuong as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate based on baguette GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database moderated by filling composition. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this estimation and cannot be defensibly summed without a direct composite measurement. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated component-weighted values where defensible USDA SR Legacy data existed. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and should be treated as approximate. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork products. Serving size of 280g is an estimate based on standard banh mi sandwich construction; actual restaurant portion may vary. Price SGD 16.48 is preserved from field survey data source record (GD002709) for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name Nem Nuong Sandwich (Banh Mi Nem Nuong) is precise and unambiguous. Vietnamese local name Bánh Mì Nem Nướng present. All mandatory Singaporean-cuisine language fields populated: English (food_name), Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros present and numeric: energy_kcal 218.0, protein_g 10.2, fat_g 9.8, carbohydrate_g 22.5. Source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component-weighted estimation, clearly documented in nutrient_source with individual FDC references and proportion rationale.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 1.4, sodium_mg 520.0, saturated_fat_g 2.9, sugar_g 2.8 — all present as numeric values, plausible for a filled baguette sandwich with pork and condiments.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 62 (numeric, greater than zero), gi_category medium, glycemic_load_per_serving 38.7, gi_source documented with University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal. Analogous estimate basis clearly stated.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (sandwich food product) used as closest_parent with gap note. FoodEx2 A00LC (Sandwich) used as closest_parent with gap note. SNOMED CT set to none with gap note explaining absence of suitable code. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters), explains what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutritional profile. Common user queries and meal context populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, medium GI and GL), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, saturated fat and sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name fields correctly set.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases list contains 15 entries covering English variants, Vietnamese script, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language is fully consistent with flat aliases list — every string in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all six hard gates pass and all eight Q criteria score 1/1 for a total of 8/8. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy with full provenance documentation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Multilingual naming satisfies Singaporean cuisine requirements (English, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese as the dish origin language. Ontology uses honest closest_parent codes with gap notes. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent between aliases_by_language and flat aliases list.
